It could not. Holmes knew it could not. Internal engineers knew it could not. Theranos was secretly running its patients' blood on conventional Walgreens machines and lying about the results. He said this while his victims included Holocaust survivor charities, hospitals, pension funds, and ordinary retirees who lost their life savings. Madoff never invested a single dollar of his clients' money — for at least 17 years. He confessed to his sons on December 10, 2008, the night before his arrest. context: ExxonMobil shareholder meeting, 2015 — reframing the question of climate action to suggest that preventing catastrophic climate change would itself cause human suffering, rather than the reverse. New York Attorney General Eric Schneiderman opened an investigation into ExxonMobil the same year for allegedly misleading investors and the public about climate risk. Viacom's own spokesman confirmed it was Redstone's voice and said he had 'made a mistake.' Lauria refused. A New York Post source close to Redstone described him as 'loving it.' Redstone was 87 years old at the time and worth approximately $5 billion. He had previously run a series of public companies — including Viacom, CBS, Paramount Pictures, MTV, BET, and Nickelodeon — while suing employees, ex-wives, ex-girlfriends, and business associates in a near-continuous stream of litigation he often described as 'my favorite sport.'
source_label: Recorded phone call (confirmed by Viacom spokesman) / NBC Today / Wikipedia / Deadline / Hollywood Reporter

title: Founder, Bad Boy Records / Music Mogul (arrested 2024)
quote: My behavior on that video is inexcusable. I take full responsibility for my actions in that video. I was disgusted then when I did it. I'm disgusted now. I'm not asking for forgiveness. I am truly sorry.
context: Instagram video apology, May 19, 2024 — posted 48 hours after CNN published hotel surveillance footage from 2016 showing Combs grabbing, throwing, kicking, stomping and dragging his then-girlfriend Cassie Ventura in a hotel hallway. For eight years Combs had denied the incident, called Ventura's lawsuit 'lies,' and his lawyers had dismissed her November 2023 allegations as 'a payday.' The apology came only after the footage made denial impossible. Cassie Ventura's attorney: 'Combs' most recent statement is more about himself than the many people he has hurt. That he was only compelled to apologize once his repeated denials were proven false shows his pathetic desperation.' In September 2024, Combs was arrested by Homeland Security on federal charges of racketeering conspiracy, sex trafficking, and transportation to engage in prostitution. Over 30 civil lawsuits subsequently filed. He is currently incarcerated at the Metropolitan Detention Center in Brooklyn awaiting trial.
source_label: Sean Combs Instagram (May 19, 2024, primary) / CNN / Billboard / NPR / Rolling Stone
disputed_note: Combs pleaded not guilty to all federal charges. The apology confirms the hotel assault. He denies all allegations of sex trafficking and the dozens of civil lawsuits. Trial pending.

title: Founder, Bad Boy Records / Music Mogul (arrested 2024)
quote: Let me be absolutely clear: I did not do any of the awful things being alleged. I will fight for my name, my family and for the truth.
context: Social media statement, December 6, 2023 — denying Cassie Ventura's lawsuit alleging rape, years of abuse, and that Combs paid $50,000 to the hotel to obtain and destroy the 2016 surveillance footage of himself beating her. Within six months CNN published that same surveillance footage and Combs issued the apology above. He was arrested in September 2024. The federal indictment accused him of 'abusing, threatening and coercing women and others around him to fulfill his sexual desires, protect his reputation, and conceal his conduct.' His companies — Bad Boy Entertainment and Combs Enterprises — were alleged to have functioned as instruments of his crimes, with employees directed to solicit drugs and sex workers, clean crime scenes, and pay for silence.
source_label: Combs social media statement (Dec 6, 2023) / NPR / Billboard / Rolling Stone / federal indictment
disputed_note: Combs pleaded not guilty. Six months after this denial, hotel surveillance footage contradicted it directly regarding the 2016 assault. Trial pending on federal sex trafficking and racketeering charges.

title: Owner, Los Angeles Clippers NBA franchise (1981–2014)
quote: Don't bring black people, and don't come.
context: Recorded private phone call with then-girlfriend V. Stiviano, released by TMZ and Deadspin, April 2014. Sterling was instructing Stiviano not to post photos on Instagram with Black people — specifically outraged by a photo of her with Magic Johnson. NBA Commissioner Adam Silver authenticated the recording and banned Sterling for life — the strongest sanction in NBA history. Sterling was simultaneously the owner of a team whose roster was over 70% Black. He received revenue, championships, and public goodwill from those players while instructing his girlfriend that Black people should not be seen at his games. President Obama: 'When ignorant folks want to advertise their ignorance, you don't really have to do anything. You just let them talk.' Sterling's lawyers unsuccessfully challenged the ban. His wife Shelly sold the team for $2 billion.
source_label: Recorded phone call (TMZ/Deadspin, Apr 2014) / authenticated by NBA / PBS / Salon / BET / NPR

title: Owner, Los Angeles Clippers NBA franchise (1981–2014)
quote: I support them and give them food, and clothes, and cars, and houses. Who gives it to them? Does someone else give it to them?
context: Same recorded call, in response to being told he has 'a whole team that's Black, that plays for you.' The framing — of NBA players as dependents who should be grateful for their owner's provision rather than employees generating billions in revenue — echoed the language of plantation ownership. Indiana Pacers forward David West tweeted: 'Sterling basically articulated Plantation Politics... Make money off the Bucks / Lay with the Women / No Association in Public good or bad.' Sterling had previously settled a $2.73 million federal housing discrimination lawsuit for refusing to rent to Black and Latino tenants, and a $2.725 million lawsuit brought by the Clippers' former GM Elgin Baylor for discrimination and wrongful termination. Despite all of this, the NAACP's Los Angeles chapter was scheduled to give Sterling a lifetime achievement award the same week this tape was released.
source_label: Recorded phone call (Deadspin extended version, Apr 2014) / Salon / NPR / BET / Code Switch

title: Founder & CEO, Papa John's Pizza (resigned 2018)
quote: Colonel Sanders called Blacks n*****s. I'm like, I've never used that word.
context: Conference call with marketing agency Laundry Service, May 22, 2018 — secretly recorded. Schnatter was referencing KFC's Colonel Sanders in what he described as an attempt to show he was not racist. The comparison had the opposite effect. The recording was released to Forbes, which published the story on July 11, 2018. Schnatter resigned as chairman the same day. Papa John's stock fell 5%. He was removed from the company's pizza boxes and its marketing. The University of Louisville removed his name from their stadium. He had previously blamed falling pizza sales on NFL players taking a knee — saying it was an NFL 'debacle' hurting his business — and had been a vocal supporter of Trump. After the resignation he stated: 'This is ridiculous. This is not who I am.'
source_label: WDRB (released audio, Apr 2021) / Forbes (Jul 11, 2018, primary) / CNBC / NPR
disputed_note: Schnatter claims the remark was made to demonstrate his opposition to racism by contrasting his own language with Colonel Sanders'. He sued Laundry Service alleging malicious leaking. The quote itself is confirmed by audio released by his own lawyers.

title: Founder & CEO, GoDaddy / Parsons Xtreme Golf
quote: These people have literally nothing and when an elephant is killed it's a big event for them, they are going to be able to eat some protein. This is no different than you or I eating beef.
context: Interview with myFoxPhoenix, April 2011 — after Parsons posted a video of himself shooting an elephant in Zimbabwe with AC/DC's 'Hell's Bells' playing over the footage of villagers in GoDaddy caps dividing the meat. When CNN's Piers Morgan asked why he didn't simply give money to the impoverished Zimbabwean villagers if that was his real concern, Parsons replied: 'I don't know that one has to do anything with the other.' He refused to take down the video and promised to post another one the following year when he planned to shoot another elephant. The video was later quietly edited to remove captions reading 'Bob Parsons fires first' and 'Bob Parsons fires again,' images of Parsons posing with the carcass, and AC/DC's soundtrack. PETA led a boycott. Scientific American noted that elephants could be deterred from crops with nothing more than beehive sounds played through speakers.
source_label: myFoxPhoenix (primary) / CNN Piers Morgan / Salon / Scientific American

title: Founder & Chairman, Fox News (resigned 2016)
quote: I think you and I should have had a sexual relationship a long time ago and then you'd be good and better and I'd be good and better.
context: Said to Gretchen Carlson in a private meeting at Fox News, recorded by Carlson on her iPhone — cited in her sexual harassment lawsuit filed July 6, 2016. Carlson had been secretly recording meetings with Ailes since 2014 after his repeated sexual comments. After the lawsuit was filed, over 20 women came forward with similar allegations. Ailes resigned from Fox News two weeks after the suit was filed. Fox News's parent company 21st Century Fox settled with Carlson for $20 million — one of the largest sexual harassment settlements in media history. Ailes had built Fox News into a $1.8 billion-per-year business. He died in May 2017, eight months after his resignation, before any criminal charges could be brought. He had also installed a secret CCTV surveillance system in Fox News offices to monitor employees, according to reporting by Gabriel Sherman.
source_label: Carlson v. Ailes lawsuit / iPhone recording cited in complaint / ABC News / Hollywood Reporter / TIME / Gabriel Sherman
disputed_note: Ailes denied all allegations. Fox settled for $20M without admission of wrongdoing. Ailes died before trial. Carlson's iPhone recordings were cited as key evidence in the lawsuit.

title: CEO, Hewlett-Packard (1999–2005) / 2016 Presidential Candidate
quote: There is no job that is America's God-given right anymore. We have to compete for jobs as a nation.
context: Statement to Congress, January 2004 — delivered as Fiorina was actively offshoring HP jobs to lower-wage countries. She laid off approximately 30,000 US workers during her six-year tenure, a process she later acknowledged: 'In the course of my time there, we laid off over 30,000 people. That's why I understand where the anger came from.' When she was fired by HP's board in 2005, the company's stock jumped 6.9% in a single day — adding nearly $3 billion in value. Her severance package was $21.4 million, including $50,000 for career counseling. HP's stock had lost nearly half its value during her tenure. She ran for US President in 2016 on the basis of her HP record.
source_label: Congressional testimony (Jan 2004) / Wikipedia / PolitiFact / CNN / WSJ

title: Chairman, Reliance Industries / India's Richest Person
quote: Growth is a way of life.
context: Recurring public statement, used across multiple speeches and Reliance Industries annual reports as Ambani's guiding philosophy — delivered while building Antilia, a 27-story, 400,000 square foot private residence in Mumbai estimated at $1–2 billion, staffed by 600 employees. The Antilia building required three helipads (requiring special aviation authority approval), houses 168 imported cars in its first six floors, and has its own temple, ballroom, snow room, and air purification system. It stands in a city where 65% of Mumbai's 18 million residents live in tenements and slums. Ambani's wealth of $99 billion equals approximately 5% of India's entire annual GDP. The land on which Antilia was built was originally an orphanage for underprivileged children — sold to Ambani in 2002 for $2.2 million when its market value was approximately $16 million, a transaction the Maharashtra Waqf Board declared in violation of law.
source_label: Reliance Industries annual reports / Wikipedia / Celebrity Net Worth / Architectural Digest

title: Founder & CEO, BlackRock (world's largest asset manager)
quote: Behaviors are going to have to change, and this is one thing we are asking companies, you have to force behaviors and sometimes force is the only way.
context: Davos panel discussion, 2022 — the CEO of the world's largest asset manager, with $10 trillion under management, declaring that corporate behavior must be forced in the direction of sustainability. Said by the man who had just sent his annual CEO letter calling for companies to plan for net-zero emissions — while BlackRock simultaneously held $85 billion in coal company stocks, was one of the largest investors in fossil fuel companies globally, and had opposed every major shareholder climate resolution brought before energy companies it invested in. Climate advocates called it 'greenflation' — using sustainability rhetoric to attract ESG investment flows while continuing to fund fossil fuel expansion. US Senator Bernie Sanders: 'While preaching sustainable investing to the world, BlackRock has invested $85 billion in coal companies.'
source_label: Davos World Economic Forum (Jan 2022) / CNBC / Guardian / Bloomberg / Common Dreams

title: Co-founder & CEO, Twitter / Founder, Square (Block)
quote: Africa will define the future, especially in terms of Bitcoin. Not sure where yet, but I'm spending one to three months in Africa in 2021 and I'm not going back.
context: Tweet, December 9, 2020 — Dorsey made this pledge to relocate to Africa for months as part of his broader promotion of Bitcoin, in which he had invested hundreds of millions of dollars of Square's treasury and his own wealth. He never completed the extended Africa stay. He did visit briefly in 2019. The Bitcoin he promoted as the 'currency of the internet' was simultaneously consuming more electricity annually than some countries, was being used to fund ransomware attacks on hospitals, schools and critical infrastructure, and would lose 75% of its value over the following two years. As Twitter CEO, Dorsey simultaneously oversaw a platform used for election interference, coordinated harassment campaigns, and the amplification of extremist movements — while tweeting about meditation retreats and silent fasting in Myanmar.
source_label: Jack Dorsey tweet (Dec 9, 2020, primary) / Reuters / Guardian / CNBC

title: Co-founder & CEO, Red Bull GmbH (died 2022)
quote: I think the virus is not the problem, the measures taken against it are. I would go back to normal life immediately. Masks have no function. And I'm in favor of targeted protection for the risk groups and otherwise normal life for everyone.
context: Austrian media interview, 2020 — the Red Bull billionaire dismissed COVID-19 measures as the real threat, not the disease itself. Mateschitz simultaneously owned Servus TV, an Austrian television network that he used as a platform for COVID-19 skeptics and lockdown critics throughout the pandemic. He invited scientists and commentators who challenged official public health guidance onto Servus TV, providing fringe views prime broadcast time. Austria had some of the highest COVID death rates in Europe during the periods when restrictions were contested. Mateschitz died in October 2022 at age 78 from prostate cancer. He had used Servus TV to build right-wing political influence in Austria throughout his life, funding coverage that critics described as aligned with the Austrian far right.
source_label: Austrian media interview (2020) / The Guardian / Der Spiegel / Reuters
disputed_note: Mateschitz's exact phrasing across interviews varied. The substance — dismissing COVID restrictions and masks as unnecessary — is consistently documented across multiple Austrian media sources.

title: CEO, Lehman Brothers (2008 collapse)
quote: I want to reach in, rip out their heart, and eat it before they know they're dead.
context: Said about short sellers targeting Lehman Brothers, 2008 — as reported in multiple accounts from colleagues present — weeks before Lehman Brothers filed for the largest bankruptcy in US history on September 15, 2008. The $613 billion collapse triggered the global financial crisis, wiped out retirement savings of millions of Americans, and required a $700 billion government bailout of the banking system. Fuld had paid himself $500 million in compensation in the eight years before the collapse. When called before Congress, he claimed not to know the firm was in danger. In 2010, he was accused by a bankruptcy examiner of participating in 'Repo 105' transactions — accounting maneuvers designed to temporarily hide $50 billion in debt from investors and regulators at the end of each quarter. No criminal charges were filed. He has not returned the $500 million.
source_label: Multiple contemporaneous accounts / Guardian / Bloomberg / Congressional testimony / PBS Frontline
disputed_note: The remark is widely reported by colleagues present. Fuld has not publicly confirmed or denied the exact phrasing. The substance of his hostility toward short sellers during this period is extensively documented.

title: CEO, Merrill Lynch (2007–2008)
quote: I do think I was responsible for saving Merrill Lynch.
context: Said after being fired by Bank of America in January 2009 — weeks after it emerged that Thain had spent $1.22 million redecorating his office at Merrill Lynch in 2008, including an $87,000 rug, a $68,000 credenza, a $35,000 'commode on legs,' and $1,405 on a 'parchment waste can.' He spent the money while Merrill was on the brink of collapse, had just received billions in government bailout funds, and had secretly accelerated $3.6 billion in executive bonuses — including his own proposed $10 million year-end bonus — before the Bank of America merger closed. The bonus acceleration was done specifically to distribute money before Bank of America's oversight took effect. BofA CEO Ken Lewis called Thain's behavior 'just a lack of the right values.' Thain repaid the $1.22 million office renovation costs.
source_label: CNBC / NYT / Bloomberg / Wall Street Journal (Jan 2009)

title: CEO, Massey Energy / Coal Industry Executive (convicted 2015)
quote: As I have said a thousand times, I don't care what people think.
context: Recurring public posture throughout Blankenship's tenure at Massey Energy — the attitude that defined his approach to mine safety regulations, worker concerns, and environmental violations. On April 5, 2010, the Upper Big Branch Mine in West Virginia — owned by Massey Energy — exploded, killing 29 coal miners in the deadliest US mine disaster in 40 years. Federal investigators found Massey had systematically falsified safety records, disabled methane monitors, and blocked safety inspections. Internal Massey emails revealed Blankenship had pressured mine managers to prioritize coal production over safety. In 2015, Blankenship was convicted of criminal conspiracy to violate mine safety laws and sentenced to one year in federal prison — the maximum for a misdemeanor. He was the first major US coal executive convicted of a crime related to a mine disaster.
source_label: Multiple press interviews / Guardian / NYT / Mine Safety Health Administration / DOJ conviction

title: Founder & CEO, Gurner Group / Luxury Property Developer
quote: We need to see pain in the economy. We need to remind people that they work for the employer, not the other way around. Unemployment has to jump 40, 50 per cent in the economy. We've got to kill that attitude — and that has to come through hurting the economy.
context: Australian Financial Review Property Summit, Sydney, September 12, 2023 — on video, to a room of property industry executives. Gurner complained that post-COVID workers had developed 'arrogance' by expecting better pay and conditions, and that the solution was mass unemployment — suggesting a 40-50% jump in the jobless rate. He said: 'Employees feel the employer is extremely lucky to have them, as opposed to the other way around. We've got to kill that attitude.' AOC shared the video on X, writing it was a 'reminder that major CEOs have skyrocketed their own pay so much that the ratio of CEO-to-worker pay is now at some of the highest levels ever recorded.' Australia's AMA president said higher unemployment was 'associated with a range of adverse health outcomes including suicide.' A Labor MP called Gurner 'self-interested.' He apologised two days later, calling the remarks 'deeply insensitive and wrong' — an apology he made only after global viral backlash. In 2017, Gurner had previously told millennials their housing affordability problems were caused by buying smashed avocado on toast and $4 coffees.
source_label: AFR Property Summit (Sep 12, 2023, on video) / Fortune / SBS News / New Statesman / Jacobin / AOC on X

title: Founder & CEO, Gurner Group / Luxury Property Developer
quote: When I was trying to buy my first home, I wasn't buying smashed avocado for $19 and four coffees at $4 each.
context: Australian '60 Minutes,' 2017 — Gurner's explanation for why millennials couldn't afford homes: their spending on small luxuries. It became one of the most mocked quotes of the decade and spawned the global 'avocado toast' discourse. Post-viral backlash, Australian Financial Review reported that Gurner had in fact received a $34,000 loan from his grandfather and backing from his former employer to start his business — not the pure bootstrap origin story his comments implied. He was then worth approximately $400M. In 2023 he returned to form at the AFR Property Summit with the mass unemployment remarks. His Gurner Group, which builds luxury apartments starting at $1M+ in Melbourne and Sydney, had a development portfolio exceeding $9.5 billion AUD by 2024.
source_label: Australian 60 Minutes (2017) / AFR / Jacobin / Fortune
disputed_note: Gurner presented himself as a self-made bootstrap success. AFR reporting revealed he received a $34,000 loan from his grandfather and backing from a former employer to start his business.
